title: Gilgamesh — Tablet XI (Utnapishtim's flood)
translation_source: R. Campbell Thompson, The Epic of Gilgamish (1928) — public domain

paragraphs.translation: Gilgamesh spoke to him, to Utnapishtim the faraway: I have looked upon you; your form is no different; you are like me. Why have the gods given to you and not to me a heart that does not fear death?

Utnapishtim said to Gilgamesh: I will reveal to you, Gilgamesh, a hidden matter, a secret of the gods I will tell you. Shuruppak, a city which you yourself know, which stands on the banks of the Euphrates — that city was old and the gods were in it.

The great gods Anu, Enlil, Ninurta, Ennugi, and Ea held counsel in heaven. Anu opened his mouth and spoke to the great gods: Has the clamor of mankind not become too loud? Their noise disturbs me; I cannot sleep because of them.

Ea opened his mouth and spoke to his servant: Man of Shuruppak, son of Ubara-Tutu, tear down your house, build a boat. Abandon possessions and seek life. Board the boat and save living things. The boat which you build — let her dimensions be equal.

On the fifth day I laid out her exterior. One acre was her floor space, ten acres each the height of her walls. I laid out six decks, dividing her into seven parts; I divided her interior into nine compartments. I drove in punting-poles and laid in supplies.

With the first glow of dawn a black cloud rose from the horizon. The gods were frightened by the flood and cowered, drawing back to the heaven of Anu. For six days and seven nights the wind and flood came. When the seventh day arrived, the flood ceased; the sea grew quiet.

I opened a hatch; light fell upon my face. I bowed and sat weeping; tears ran down my cheeks. I looked at the world: all mankind had returned to clay. I offered a sacrifice on the mountain peak. The gods smelled the savor; the gods gathered like flies over the sacrificer.

When Enlil arrived and saw the boat, he was filled with wrath against the Igigi gods: Has any of the flesh escaped? No man was to survive the destruction! Ea opened his mouth and spoke: How could you send the flood without counsel? Let Utnapishtim and his wife be taken faraway; let them dwell at the mouth of the rivers.

Then Enlil went up into the boat and seized my hand, making me and my wife kneel. He touched our foreheads and standing between us, blessed us: Formerly Utnapishtim was mortal; now Utnapishtim and his wife shall be like us, the gods; Utnapishtim shall dwell faraway at the mouth of the rivers.

Utnapishtim said to him, to Gilgamesh: The gods once granted eternal life to one man — will they do so for you? The fate of mankind overtakes you; sleep and death are one. This is the story of how I obtained life everlasting; now you must find the plant of heart renewal if you can.
